title: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition
authors: L. John Hoffer, Bruce R. Bistrian

items.title: When to Initiate Specialized Nutritional Support (SNS) for Hospitalized Adults
items.content: Hospitalized adult patients require SNS when they cannot achieve adequate nutrition through optimized voluntary nutritional support. The decision involves screening, diagnosing the severity of nutritional disorders, and determining the urgency within the clinical context.
keyPoints: Three types of SNS: optimized voluntary nutritional support, instrumental enteral nutrition (EN), parenteral nutrition (PN)
Screening and diagnosis are crucial first steps
Assess severity and urgency in the overall clinical context
Collaborate with dietitians and specialized pharmacists due to lack of formal training
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition

items.title: Selection and Administration of Specialized Nutritional Support (SNS)
items.content: Selecting the appropriate SNS involves understanding nutritional physiology, nutrient requirements, pathophysiology, and diagnosis of nutritional disorders. It also requires knowledge of indications, advantages, risks, and administration details for each type of SNS.
keyPoints: Three steps in managing nutritional disorders: screening/diagnosis, severity/urgency assessment, selection of SNS
Physicians should understand nutritional physiology and pathophysiology
Collaboration with clinical dietitians and pharmacists is recommended
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition

items.title: Understanding Energy Expenditure in Clinical Nutrition
items.content: Energy expenditure is crucial for determining dietary needs, especially in clinical settings where patients may have altered metabolic demands due to illness or therapy.
keyPoints: Total daily energy expenditure of a healthy sedentary adult is ~36 kcal/kg.
Resting energy expenditure accounts for ~75% of total energy expenditure.
Energy balance affects protein requirements; positive energy balance can improve body protein retention, while negative energy balance increases dietary protein needs.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · NUTRITIONAL PHYSIOLOGY (See Chaps. 343-345.) (part 1)

items.title: Protein Requirements and Catabolism in Clinical Conditions
items.content: Protein intake must be adjusted based on metabolic demands, particularly in conditions that increase protein catabolism or loss.
keyPoints: Minimum dietary protein requirement for a healthy adult is 0.65 g/kg; recommended intake is 0.80 g/kg.
Highly protein-catabolic patients may excrete >15 g N/d, indicating significant protein and tissue mass loss.
Critically ill patients often require 1.2–2.0 g protein/kg of normal body weight per day.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · NUTRITIONAL PHYSIOLOGY (See Chaps. 343-345.) (part 1)

items.title: Energy-Protein Interactions and Nutritional Strategies
items.content: Balancing energy and protein intake is essential to mitigate muscle wasting, especially in the context of systemic inflammation or negative energy balance.
keyPoints: Systemic inflammation reduces but does not eliminate the benefits of increased protein under hypocaloric conditions.
A minimum energy dose (~50% of total energy expenditure) may help preserve active tissue during severe inflammation.
Energy doses >70% of total energy expenditure do not increase protein-sparing effects and can lead to adverse outcomes.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · NUTRITIONAL PHYSIOLOGY (See Chaps. 343-345.) (part 1)

items.title: Importance of Monitoring Micronutrient Levels in Hospitalized Patients
items.content: Hospitalized patients often experience overt deficiencies of potassium, sodium, magnesium, and phosphorus due to common gastrointestinal or renal losses. It is crucial to monitor and correct these levels as standard practice.
keyPoints: Overt deficiencies of potassium, sodium, magnesium, and phosphorus are common in hospitalized patients.
Standard practice involves monitoring and correcting these micronutrient levels.
Drugs can cause renal losses of potassium, magnesium, or zinc, necessitating increased provision.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · NUTRITIONAL PHYSIOLOGY (See Chaps. 343-345.) (part 2)

items.title: Impact of Systemic Inflammation on Micronutrient Status Evaluation
items.content: Systemic inflammation complicates the evaluation of micronutrient status by increasing requirements and redistributing nutrients like iron, selenium, and zinc into tissue stores. This reduces the reliability of standard laboratory tests.
keyPoints: Systemic inflammation increases requirements for certain micronutrients.
Inflammation causes redistribution of nutrients such as iron, selenium, and zinc into tissues.
Standard lab tests become less reliable due to these changes.
Assessment should include previous/current intake, health status, and potential excessive loss routes.
Use serum albumin or C-reactive protein levels to gauge inflammation intensity.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · NUTRITIONAL PHYSIOLOGY (See Chaps. 343-345.) (part 2)

items.title: Understanding Starvation-Related Malnutrition (SRM) and Its Physiologic Impact
items.content: Starvation-related malnutrition (SRM) is characterized by prolonged semi-starvation leading to significant muscle loss, including cardiac and respiratory muscles. This condition results in impaired physiologic functions such as lethargy, hypothermia risk, and functional disability. SRM's cardinal anatomic features are generalized muscle loss and subcutaneous adipose tissue depletion, detectable through physical examination.
keyPoints: SRM manifests as weight loss with potential starvation edema due to increased extracellular fluid volume.
A 25% reduction in body weight significantly compromises physiologic function; a 50% reduction places young adults on the cusp of thermodynamic survival.
Causes include involuntary food deprivation, psychological factors, and physical barriers to eating.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · MACRONUTRIENT MALNUTRITION SYNDROMES

items.title: Chronic Disease-Related Malnutrition (CDM) and Its Complications
items.content: Chronic disease-related malnutrition (CDM) is SRM compounded by chronic systemic inflammation, common in patients with chronic infections, autoimmune diseases, or neoplastic diseases. CDM exacerbates muscle loss, weakness, fatigue, and impairs adaptation to starvation, creating a cycle of worsening condition. Nutritional intervention can address the anorexia-driven inadequate food consumption that drives CDM.
keyPoints: CDM is characterized by moderate systemic inflammation and generalized muscle loss.
Anorexia, worsened by chronic disease, significantly contributes to nutritional deficits in CDM.
Nutritional interventions are crucial for managing CDM while treating the primary disease.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · MACRONUTRIENT MALNUTRITION SYNDROMES

items.title: Acute Disease-Related Malnutrition (ADM) and Its Urgency
items.content: Acute disease-related malnutrition (ADM), also known as injury-induced malnutrition, occurs due to severe tissue injury or sepsis. It leads to rapid muscle loss and increased resting energy expenditure when voluntary food intake is compromised. Without effective treatment of the underlying condition and supportive nutritional therapy, death from ADM can occur much sooner than from untreated SRM.
keyPoints: ADM involves rapid and severe generalized muscle loss due to metabolic-inflammatory responses.
Voluntary food intake is usually impossible during ADM, necessitating immediate medical intervention.
Death from untreated ADM occurs more rapidly compared to simple starvation in nonobese adults.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · MACRONUTRIENT MALNUTRITION SYNDROMES

items.title: Recognizing Starvation Disease: Muscle Mass and Causes
items.content: Generalized muscle loss is a key indicator of starvation disease, which can be overlooked despite its obvious signs. It is crucial to consider treatable causes such as SRM and CDM when generalized muscle loss is identified.
keyPoints: Muscle tissue accounts for ~80% of an adult's active tissue mass.
Generalized muscle loss has multiple causes including sarcopenia, disease atrophy, glucocorticoid therapy, endocrine diseases, and primary muscle or neuromuscular diseases.
SRM and CDM are common treatable causes of muscle loss.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · NUTRITIONAL DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.title: BMI in Diagnosing Starvation Disease
items.content: While BMI is a useful tool, it should not be the sole criterion for diagnosing starvation disease. Visual assessment of muscle mass can provide more accurate insights, especially in patients with residual obesity or edema.
keyPoints: Normal BMI range: 20–25 kg/m²
BMI <19–20 indicates nonpathologically reduced muscle and fat mass.
BMI <15 suggests severe starvation disease; BMI <13 is often incompatible with life, particularly in older patients.
A BMI >17 does not rule out starvation disease due to possible residual obesity or expanded ECF volume.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · NUTRITIONAL DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.title: Differentiating SRM and CDM through Systemic Inflammation
items.content: The presence of systemic inflammation is a distinguishing factor between SRM and CDM. Key laboratory indicators include serum albumin concentration and serum C-reactive protein levels.
keyPoints: Systemic inflammation distinguishes SRM from CDM.
Reduced serum albumin and increased serum C-reactive protein indicate systemic inflammation.
Inflammation increases ECF volume, reducing serum albumin concentration.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · NUTRITIONAL DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.title: Hypoalbuminemia as an Indicator of Systemic Inflammation, Not Malnutrition
items.content: Hypoalbuminemia and reduced serum prealbumin are often misinterpreted as signs of malnutrition. However, these proteins act as negative acute-phase reactants that primarily indicate systemic inflammation. This inflammation can lead to increased muscle catabolism and anorexia, which may increase the risk of Cachexia-related Diseases (CDM), but does not confirm its presence or development.
keyPoints: Hypoalbuminemia and reduced serum prealbumin are negative acute-phase reactants indicating systemic inflammation rather than malnutrition.
Systemic inflammation can cause anorexia and muscle catabolism, increasing the risk of Cachexia-related Diseases (CDM).
Serum acute-phase reactants remain abnormal as long as systemic inflammation persists.
Appropriate nutrition may improve serum albumin concentration but will not normalize until after inflammation subsides due to albumin’s half-life of at least 2 weeks.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · NUTRITIONAL DIAGNOSIS (part 2)

items.title: Assessing Protein-Catabolic Intensity and Nutritional Risk
items.content: Protein-catabolic disease is characterized by generalized muscle loss. The intensity of protein catabolism can be assessed by measuring body nitrogen (N) loss, primarily through urinary urea N. A validated formula estimates daily total N loss as: N loss (g) = g N in urinary urea/0.85 + 2. Patients with depleted muscle stores are at higher risk despite lower daily N loss due to their proximity to lethal depletion.
keyPoints: Protein-catabolic disease is marked by generalized muscle loss.
Body nitrogen loss can be estimated using the formula: N loss (g) = g N in urinary urea/0.85 + 2.
Urinary urea N accounts for ~85% of urinary nitrogen.
Muscle-depleted patients are at greater risk due to proximity to lethal depletion, despite lower daily nitrogen loss.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · NUTRITIONAL DIAGNOSIS (part 2)

items.title: Why Opt for Enteral Nutrition?
items.content: Enteral Nutrition (EN) is the preferred method when optimized voluntary nutritional support fails or is not feasible, provided the patient's gastrointestinal tract is functional and accessible.
keyPoints: EN maintains digestive, absorptive, and immunologic functions of the GI tract.
It is safe, simple, and cost-effective compared to parenteral nutrition.
Used when voluntary nutritional support cannot meet needs.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · SPECIALIZED NUTRITIONAL SUPPORT (part 1)

items.title: Characteristics of Standard Polymeric Formulas
items.content: Standard polymeric formulas are the most widely used EN products, designed to meet the nutritional requirements of a normal, healthy person.
keyPoints: Carbohydrates provide most energy; proteins are intact and require adequate pancreatic and intestinal enzyme function.
Isotonic or nearly so; provides 1000–2000 kcal and 50–70 g protein/L.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · SPECIALIZED NUTRITIONAL SUPPORT (part 1)

items.title: Advantages of Protein-Enriched Formulas
items.content: Protein-enriched formulas are used to meet the increased protein requirements without energy overfeeding, especially for protein-catabolic patients.
keyPoints: Provide ~90 g protein and 1000 kcal/L.
Originally marketed for weight-reducing obese patients; now used for protein-catabolic patients.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · SPECIALIZED NUTRITIONAL SUPPORT (part 1)

items.title: Energy Concentration of Dextrose Monohydrate in Parenteral Nutrition
items.content: Dextrose monohydrate is used in parenteral nutrition, and its hydration state reduces the energy concentration from 4.0 kcal/g to 3.4 kcal/g.
keyPoints: Dextrose monohydrate provides 3.4 kcal/g due to its hydrated form.
This reduction in energy concentration is important for calculating total caloric intake.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · SPECIALIZED NUTRITIONAL SUPPORT (part 2)

items.title: Lipid Emulsion Composition and Infusion Rates
items.content: Mixed lipid emulsions, including medium-chain triglycerides, n-9 monounsaturated fatty acids, and n-3 fatty acids, are preferred over traditional soybean oil-based emulsions. Standard lipid infusion rates should not exceed 8 g/h.
keyPoints: Standard lipid infusion rate: ≤8 g/h
Equivalent to 175 g (1925 kcal)/d for a 70-kg patient
Mixed emulsions reduce the risk of infections and length of stay in critically ill patients.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · SPECIALIZED NUTRITIONAL SUPPORT (part 2)

items.subtopic: Indications, Selection, and Provision of Specialized Nutritional Support
items.title: When to Consider Specialized Nutritional Support (SNS)
items.content: Most hospitalized patients do not need SNS as they can manage their nutritional needs with appropriate management of their primary disease. However, those unable to eat or digest enough from the standard diet and at risk for severe malnutrition should be considered for optimized voluntary support. Instrumental SNS is indicated when voluntary methods are impractical or have failed.
keyPoints: SNS not usually needed if patients can manage nutrition with primary disease management
Candidates for SNS include those unable to eat/digest standard diet and at risk of severe malnutrition (SRM, CDM)
Instrumental SNS is indicated when voluntary methods are impractical or have failed
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · Indications, Selection, and Provision of Specialized Nutritional Support

items.subtopic: Indications, Selection, and Provision of Specialized Nutritional Support
items.title: Criteria for Choosing Between Enteral Nutrition (EN) and Parenteral Nutrition (PN)
items.content: The decision between EN and PN involves evaluating if nutrient ingestion will remain inadequate for days, presence of important muscle loss, increased nutrient requirements due to conditions like inflammatory diarrhea or fistulas, and whether SNS can improve clinical outcomes or quality of life.
keyPoints: Evaluate if nutrient intake will be inadequate for many days
Assess for significant muscle loss from any cause
Consider increased nutrient needs due to conditions like inflammatory diarrhea, enterocutaneous fistulas, or exudates
Judgment that SNS can improve clinical outcomes or quality of life
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · Indications, Selection, and Provision of Specialized Nutritional Support

items.title: Indications for Enteral Nutrition
items.content: Enteral nutrition is indicated when a patient cannot consume adequate food voluntarily due to conditions like impaired consciousness, severe dysphagia, or mechanical ventilation. It's also considered in cases of anorexia, nausea, vomiting, and other factors that limit voluntary intake.
keyPoints: Indicated for patients unable to eat enough food long-term with a functional gastrointestinal tract.
Commonly used for impaired consciousness, severe dysphagia, or mechanical ventilation.
Considered when voluntary intake is limited by anorexia, nausea, vomiting, pain, etc.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · EN THERAPY (part 1)

items.title: Contraindications and Relative Contraindications for EN
items.content: Enteral nutrition is contraindicated in cases of intestinal ischemia, mechanical obstruction, peritonitis, and gastrointestinal hemorrhage. High-dose pressor therapy poses a relative contraindication due to the risk of intestinal ischemic injury.
keyPoints: Contraindications include intestinal ischemia, mechanical obstruction, peritonitis, and GI hemorrhage.
High-dose pressor therapy is a relative contraindication.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · EN THERAPY (part 1)

items.title: Initiation and Monitoring of Enteral Nutrition
items.content: Enteral nutrition can be initiated with nasogastric tube feeding if gastric contractility is adequate (<1200 mL/d output) and no intraabdominal pathology is present. The patient's bed should be elevated to at least 30 degrees to reduce regurgitation risk.
keyPoints: Initiate EN when GI function is adequate (gastric output <1200 mL/d).
Elevate the head of the bed to at least $30^{	ext{o}}$ to minimize regurgitation.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · EN THERAPY (part 1)

items.title: Monitoring and Managing Blood Glucose in Enteral Nutrition
items.content: Blood glucose concentrations should be monitored regularly during enteral nutrition (EN) therapy. Additional measures, such as intravenous fluid, electrolyte, and insulin therapy, may be required to maintain homeostasis due to the inability of standard EN products to manage abnormal fluid volume, electrolyte, and mineral requirements.
keyPoints: Standard EN products do not manage abnormal fluid, electrolyte, and mineral needs.
Blood glucose concentrations should be monitored regularly during EN therapy.
Additional measures may include intravenous fluids, electrolytes, and insulin to maintain homeostasis.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · EN THERAPY (part 2)

items.title: Challenges of Meeting Nutritional Goals in the ICU
items.content: In the intensive care unit (ICU), enteral nutrition is crucial for meeting macronutrient requirements and maintaining intestinal barrier and immunologic functions. Despite guidelines recommending early initiation post-fluid resuscitation, EN often falls short of protein targets. High-protein EN products or powdered supplements can help address this shortfall.
keyPoints: EN in ICU serves to meet macronutrient needs and maintain intestinal function.
Guidelines recommend starting EN soon after fluid resuscitation and stabilization.
EN often fails to reach protein provision targets, even after extended periods.
High-protein EN products or powdered supplements can help correct protein shortfalls.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · EN THERAPY (part 2)

items.title: When to Initiate Parenteral Nutrition
items.content: Parenteral nutrition should be initiated soon after the patient has been hemodynamically stabilized, with established glucose, electrolyte, and acid-base homeostasis. It is crucial for patients who cannot tolerate enteral feeding due to specific medical conditions.
keyPoints: Initiate PN after hemodynamic stabilization and establishment of metabolic balance.
Avoid providing energy equal to total expenditure in the first week; it can be harmful under certain conditions.
Initial glucose dose should not exceed 200 g/d to prevent hyperglycemia.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · PN THERAPY (part 1)

items.title: Glucose Management in Parenteral Nutrition
items.content: Proper management of glucose infusion is critical in PN to avoid complications such as hyperglycemia and refeeding syndrome. Monitoring and adjusting insulin dosage are essential components.
keyPoints: Most non-critically ill patients require ≤500 g glucose/d (1700 kcal/d) assuming a 70 kg body weight.
Patients with acute disease manifestations should not receive >350 g glucose/d during critical illness phases.
Capillary blood glucose concentrations are monitored several times daily, aiming for <140 mg/dL and >80 mg/dL.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · PN THERAPY (part 1)

items.title: Biochemical Monitoring in Parenteral Nutrition
items.content: Regular biochemical monitoring is essential to ensure patient safety and adjust PN components as needed. This includes tracking serum levels of various substances before and during PN.
keyPoints: Measure serum urea, creatinine, electrolytes, glucose, magnesium, phosphate, calcium, albumin prior to starting PN.
Monitor triglycerides and liver function indicators after initiating PN to assess lipid infusion tolerance.
Use N balance determination to evaluate protein catabolism severity in patients with critical disease manifestations.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · PN THERAPY (part 1)

items.title: Criteria for Discontinuing Parenteral Nutrition
items.content: Parenteral nutrition should be tapered and discontinued when a patient can meet one-half to two-thirds of their nutritional requirements via the enteral route without mechanical or other barriers. Transitioning to oral nutrition is preferred over replacing PN with EN.
keyPoints: PN should be terminated once a patient can tolerate 1/2 to 2/3 of their requirement by enteral route.
Optimized voluntary nutrition is safer and more effective than replacing PN with EN for transitioning patients.
Discontinuing PN too early may induce anxiety and recreate starvation conditions, delaying full oral food consumption.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · PN THERAPY (part 2)

items.title: Managing Hyperglycemia in Parenteral Nutrition
items.content: Hyperglycemia is a common complication of PN, especially in patients with insulin resistance. It can be managed by infusing hypocaloric glucose and using intravenous lipids to meet energy needs.
keyPoints: Hyperglycemia occurs frequently due to insulin resistance from conditions like diabetes or high-dose glucocorticoids.
Maintain glucose concentrations <140 mg/dL with minimal risk of hypoglycemia by infusing hypocaloric glucose and using intravenous lipids if necessary.
Use the lowest possible insulin dose to minimize hyperinsulinemia and reduce hypoglycemia risk.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · PN THERAPY (part 2)

items.title: Preventing Volume Overload in Parenteral Nutrition
items.content: Volume overload is a significant complication of PN, which can be prevented by careful monitoring and adjusting fluid provision to not exceed 2 L/d unless necessary.
keyPoints: Total fluid provision should generally not exceed 2 L/d without high losses.
Hypertonic intravenous glucose increases insulin response and sodium/water retention, contributing to volume overload.
Prevent volume overload by using a compounder for PN solutions, infusing glucose at the lowest rate, avoiding energy overfeeding, and monitoring volume balance.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · PN THERAPY (part 2)

items.title: Guidelines for Starting Parenteral Nutrition in ICU
items.content: Parenteral nutrition (PN) should be considered after enteral nutrition (EN) has been initiated and if the protein goal is not met within 7-10 days, especially if the patient remains in a protein-catabolic state.
keyPoints: Start EN soon after stabilization and establish enteral access to a functioning gastrointestinal tract.
Consider PN if the protein goal is unmet after 7–10 days of EN.
Avoid soy-based lipid emulsions during the first week of PN.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · PN THERAPY (part 3) | SPECIAL CLINICAL SITUATIONS (part 1)

items.title: Iron Deficiency in Hospitalized Patients
items.content: Iron deficiency is common among hospitalized patients and should be evaluated early using serum ferritin and fasting serum transferrin saturation, with a transferrin saturation <20% indicating functional iron deficiency.
keyPoints: Common causes of iron deficiency include preexisting deficiency, insufficient food intake, gastrointestinal blood loss, and repeated blood sampling.
Serum ferritin is often confounded by anemia of systemic inflammation.
Fasting serum transferrin saturation <20% indicates functional iron deficiency.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · PN THERAPY (part 3) | SPECIAL CLINICAL SITUATIONS (part 1)

items.title: Challenges and Considerations in Nutritional Support for Critically Ill Patients
items.content: Nutritional support regimens should be personalized, considering the patient's anatomic and metabolic condition, avoiding energy overfeeding, and focusing on protein delivery to mitigate muscle loss.
keyPoints: Avoid matching energy provision to expenditure during severe systemic inflammation.
Guide protein or amino acid dosing based on physiological reasoning and individual evaluation.
Adopt a long-view approach to nutritional support that anticipates post-critical illness recovery.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · PN THERAPY (part 3) | SPECIAL CLINICAL SITUATIONS (part 1)

items.title: Intravenous Iron and Infection Risk
items.content: Intravenous iron should be delayed if there is known or suspected bacterial infection, particularly during the intense phase of acute disease management (ADM), due to increased susceptibility to infections.
keyPoints: Delay intravenous iron in presence of bacterial infection.
Increased serum iron can heighten risk for gram-negative and other microbial infections.
Iron may catalyze free radical formation, intensifying catabolic response.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · SPECIAL CLINICAL SITUATIONS (part 2)

items.title: Zinc Requirements in High-Volume Diarrhea
items.content: Patients with intestinal fistulas or high-volume chronic diarrhea require additional zinc supplementation to avoid deficiency.
keyPoints: One liter of secretory diarrhea contains ~12 mg of zinc.
Daily requirement is 15 mg; patients need an additional 12 mg for losses.
Parenteral zinc bioavailability is low: 12 mg parenteral equals 30 mg oral.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · SPECIAL CLINICAL SITUATIONS (part 2)

items.title: Nutritional Support in Liver Failure
items.content: Patients with severe hepatic disease should receive generous nutritional support despite the risk of hepatic encephalopathy, with careful management to mitigate risks.
keyPoints: Severe hepatic disease patients are intolerant to starvation and often have CDM.
SNS should be generous in energy and protein.
Mitigate encephalopathy risk by managing fluid balance, acid-base status, and spreading protein intake.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · SPECIAL CLINICAL SITUATIONS (part 2)

items.title: Understanding Refeeding Syndrome: Mechanisms and Management
items.content: Refeeding syndrome occurs when nutritional repletion is introduced too rapidly in patients with severe malnutrition, leading to metabolic disturbances. Carbohydrate intake stimulates insulin secretion, causing antinatriuretic effects that expand extracellular fluid volume, especially with excessive sodium. This can lead to edema and electrolyte imbalances such as hypophosphatemia, which impairs muscle function and may cause hemolysis.
keyPoints: Occurs in the first week of nutritional repletion when carbohydrate and sodium are introduced too rapidly
Carbohydrate stimulates insulin secretion causing antinatriuretic effects and ECF volume expansion
Severe limitation of sodium and slow increase of carbohydrates can minimize edema
Hypophosphatemia, hypokalemia, and hypomagnesemia occur and are dangerous; frequent serum concentration measurements and supplementation are necessary
Potential complications include left heart failure due to increased intravascular volume, cardiac demand on atrophic ventricles, and myocardial deficiencies of potassium, phosphorus, or magnesium
Cardiac arrhythmias may occur
Acute thiamine deficiency encephalopathy is a preventable complication
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · SPECIAL CLINICAL SITUATIONS (part 3) | REFEEDING SYNDROME

items.title: Preventing Refeeding Syndrome: Key Strategies
items.content: To prevent refeeding syndrome, it is crucial to manage the rate of carbohydrate and sodium introduction carefully. Limiting sodium intake and gradually increasing carbohydrates can help minimize complications such as edema. Monitoring serum concentrations of phosphorus, potassium, and magnesium frequently allows for timely supplementation to avoid dangerous deficiencies.
keyPoints: Severely limit sodium provision
Increase carbohydrate provision slowly
Frequent measurement of serum phosphate, potassium, and magnesium levels is essential
Provide appropriate supplements based on frequent measurements
Monitor for signs of left heart failure and cardiac arrhythmias
Prevent acute thiamine deficiency encephalopathy with appropriate supplementation
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · SPECIAL CLINICAL SITUATIONS (part 3) | REFEEDING SYNDROME

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a type of specialized nutritional support (SNS)?
items.explanation: Oral nutritional supplementation is not listed as a type of specialized nutritional support in the passage.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition

items.question: What is instrumental enteral nutrition (EN) characterized by?
options: Delivery of nutrients through an intravenous line
Use of a liquid nutrient formula delivered via tube to the stomach or small intestine
items.explanation: Instrumental enteral nutrition involves delivering a liquid nutrient formula through a tube placed in the stomach or small intestine.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition

items.question: What is the first step in managing nutritional disorders in hospitalized patients?
items.explanation: The management of nutritional disorders begins with screening and diagnosis.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition

items.question: What is the average daily minimum protein requirement for a healthy adult consuming an otherwise adequate diet?
items.explanation: The average daily minimum protein requirement is 0.65 g/kg.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · NUTRITIONAL PHYSIOLOGY (See Chaps. 343-345.) (part 1)

items.question: Which method can be used to measure resting energy expenditure?
items.explanation: Resting energy expenditure can be measured by indirect calorimetry.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · NUTRITIONAL PHYSIOLOGY (See Chaps. 343-345.) (part 1)

items.question: What is the effect of positive energy balance achieved through greater carbohydrate consumption on body protein retention?
options: It has no effect on protein retention
items.explanation: Positive energy balance can improve body protein retention.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · NUTRITIONAL PHYSIOLOGY (See Chaps. 343-345.) (part 1)

items.question: What is the recommended daily protein intake for critically ill patients?
items.explanation: The common recommendation for critically ill patients is 1.2–2.0 g protein/kg of normal body weight per day.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · NUTRITIONAL PHYSIOLOGY (See Chaps. 343-345.) (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following micronutrients are commonly monitored and corrected in hospitalized patients due to their frequent overt deficiencies?
items.explanation: Overt deficiencies of potassium, sodium, magnesium, and phosphorus are common in hospitalized patients.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · NUTRITIONAL PHYSIOLOGY (See Chaps. 343-345.) (part 2)

items.question: What is a consequence of systemic inflammation on the clinical laboratory evaluation of micronutrient status?
options: It decreases the requirements for certain micronutrients.
It increases the reliability of standard laboratory tests.
It redistributes nutrients into tissue stores, reducing test reliability.
It eliminates the need to monitor serum concentrations of albumin or C-reactive protein.
items.explanation: Systemic inflammation partially redistributes certain micronutrients into tissue stores, reducing the reliability of many standard laboratory tests.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · NUTRITIONAL PHYSIOLOGY (See Chaps. 343-345.) (part 2)

items.question: Which serum concentration is particularly useful for determining the intensity of systemic inflammation due to its wide dynamic range and short half-life?
items.explanation: Serum concentrations of C-reactive protein are particularly useful for determining the intensity of systemic inflammation.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · NUTRITIONAL PHYSIOLOGY (See Chaps. 343-345.) (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a cause of Starvation-Related Malnutrition (SRM) in hospitalized patients?
items.explanation: Chronic systemic inflammation is associated with Chronic Disease-Related Malnutrition (CDM), not SRM.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · MACRONUTRIENT MALNUTRITION SYNDROMES

items.question: What physiological change occurs as the body adapts to sustained inadequate energy provision?
options: Reduction in energy expenditure and net protein catabolism
items.explanation: The body reduces energy expenditure and net protein catabolism to adapt to inadequate energy provision.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · MACRONUTRIENT MALNUTRITION SYNDROMES

items.question: What is a cardinal anatomic feature of Starvation-Related Malnutrition (SRM)?
items.explanation: Generalized muscle loss and subcutaneous adipose tissue depletion are cardinal features of SRM.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · MACRONUTRIENT MALNUTRITION SYNDROMES

items.question: Which condition is characterized by a metabolic syndrome of moderate systemic inflammation and unrelenting generalized muscle loss?
items.explanation: Cachexia is characterized by moderate systemic inflammation and unrelenting generalized muscle loss.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · MACRONUTRIENT MALNUTRITION SYNDROMES

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a cause of generalized muscle loss?
items.explanation: Adequate protein and energy provision is a treatment strategy, not a cause of muscle loss.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · NUTRITIONAL DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.question: What does a BMI <15 indicate?
options: Nonpathologically reduced muscle and fat mass
items.explanation: A BMI <15 indicates severe starvation disease.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · NUTRITIONAL DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.question: What is the normal reference range for Body Mass Index (BMI)?
items.explanation: The normal reference range for BMI is 20–25 kg/m².
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · NUTRITIONAL DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.question: Which laboratory indicator is NOT useful in distinguishing SRM from CDM?
options: Bedside ultrasound for muscle mass quantification
items.explanation: Bedside ultrasound is useful for quantifying muscle mass but not for distinguishing SRM from CDM.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · NUTRITIONAL DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.question: Which statement best describes the relationship between hypoalbuminemia and systemic inflammation?
options: Hypoalbuminemia is a direct indicator of malnutrition.
Hypoalbuminemia indicates the presence of systemic inflammation, not necessarily malnutrition.
Hypoalbuminemia can be normalized immediately with appropriate nutrition.
Hypoalbuminemia has no relation to systemic inflammation.
items.explanation: Hypoalbuminemia is a negative acute-phase reactant indicating systemic inflammation, not directly malnutrition.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · NUTRITIONAL DIAGNOSIS (part 2)

items.question: What does the formula N loss (g) = g N in urinary urea/0.85 + 2 estimate?
items.explanation: The formula estimates daily total nitrogen loss.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · NUTRITIONAL DIAGNOSIS (part 2)

items.question: Why should the rate of nitrogen loss be interpreted in the context of existing muscle mass?
options: Because patients with preserved muscle store lose more nitrogen per day.
Because protein-catabolic intensity is unrelated to muscle mass.
Because patients closer to lethal depletion are at greater risk despite lower daily nitrogen loss.
Because nitrogen loss does not affect clinical outcomes.
items.explanation: Patients with less muscle mass are at greater risk of disease progression due to proximity to lethal depletion, even if their nitrogen loss rate is lower.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · NUTRITIONAL DIAGNOSIS (part 2)

items.question: What is the primary advantage of optimized voluntary nutritional support?
options: It engages and empowers the patient.
It is inexpensive and simple to administer.
It requires minimal time and labor.
It provides a fixed nutrient composition.
items.explanation: Optimized voluntary nutritional support engages and empowers the patient, encouraging mobilization and reconditioning.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · SPECIALIZED NUTRITIONAL SUPPORT (part 1)

items.question: When is enteral nutrition (EN) usually considered as a treatment option?
options: When optimized voluntary nutritional support is feasible.
When the patient's gastrointestinal tract cannot be accessed.
When optimized voluntary nutritional support is impossible or has failed.
items.explanation: Enteral Nutrition is considered when optimized voluntary nutritional support is not possible or fails, and the GI tract can be accessed.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · SPECIALIZED NUTRITIONAL SUPPORT (part 1)

items.question: Which type of enteral nutrition formulas are most widely used?
items.explanation: Standard polymeric formulas are the most widely used enteral nutrition products, designed to meet the nutritional requirements of a normal, healthy person.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · SPECIALIZED NUTRITIONAL SUPPORT (part 1)

items.question: What is the main reason for using protein-enriched formulas in enteral nutrition?
options: To meet increased protein requirements of weight-reducing obese patients.
To improve bowel function and feeding tolerance.
items.explanation: Protein-enriched formulas were originally marketed to meet the increased protein requirement of weight-reducing obese patients.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · SPECIALIZED NUTRITIONAL SUPPORT (part 1)

items.question: What is the energy concentration of dextrose monohydrate used in parenteral nutrition?
items.explanation: Dextrose monohydrate has an energy concentration of 3.4 kcal/g due to its hydration state.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · SPECIALIZED NUTRITIONAL SUPPORT (part 2)

items.question: Which type of fatty acids are more prone to lipid peroxidation in traditional soybean oil-based emulsions?
items.explanation: Soybean oil is more prone to lipid peroxidation due to its n-6 polyunsaturated fatty acids.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · SPECIALIZED NUTRITIONAL SUPPORT (part 2)

items.question: What is the maximum standard lipid infusion rate for a 70-kg patient?
items.explanation: The standard lipid infusion rate should not exceed 8 g/h, which equates to 175 g (1925 kcal)/d for a 70-kg patient.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · SPECIALIZED NUTRITIONAL SUPPORT (part 2)

items.subtopic: Indications, Selection, and Provision of Specialized Nutritional Support
items.question: Which factor is NOT considered when deciding to provide enteral nutrition (EN) or parenteral nutrition (PN)?
options: The patient's nutrient requirements are increased.
There is important muscle loss of any cause.
Nutrient ingestion will likely continue to be inadequate for many days.
The patient has a terminal disease whose downward course will not be mitigated by SNS.
items.explanation: Terminal disease with no mitigation from SNS is not a factor in deciding EN or PN.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · Indications, Selection, and Provision of Specialized Nutritional Support

items.subtopic: Indications, Selection, and Provision of Specialized Nutritional Support
items.question: When is instrumental specialized nutritional support (SNS) indicated?
options: When optimized voluntary nutritional support is appropriate.
When the patient can eat enough with primary disease management.
When optimized voluntary nutritional support is inappropriate or has failed.
Only when there is important muscle loss.
items.explanation: Instrumental SNS is indicated when optimized voluntary support is inappropriate, impractical, or has failed.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · Indications, Selection, and Provision of Specialized Nutritional Support

items.subtopic: Indications, Selection, and Provision of Specialized Nutritional Support
items.question: Which condition does NOT warrant consideration for specialized nutritional support (SNS)?
options: The patient can eat enough with primary disease management.
The patient is at high risk of developing SRM or CDM.
The patient has important muscle loss.
The patient's nutrient requirements are increased.
items.explanation: Patients who can eat enough with appropriate management do not require SNS.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · Indications, Selection, and Provision of Specialized Nutritional Support

items.question: Which of the following is NOT an absolute contraindication for enteral nutrition (EN)?
items.explanation: Severe coagulopathy is not an absolute contraindication but increases the risk of complications.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · EN THERAPY (part 1)

items.question: What is a common complication associated with enteral nutrition?
items.explanation: Aspiration of regurgitated or vomited formula can lead to aspiration pneumonia.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · EN THERAPY (part 1)

items.question: When is enteral nutrition indicated?
options: When the gastrointestinal tract is non-functional
When voluntary nutrition is optimized and appropriate
When the patient cannot eat enough food for a long time
When high-dose pressor therapy is required
items.explanation: EN is indicated when the patient is unable to eat enough food and voluntary nutrition is impossible or inappropriate.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · EN THERAPY (part 1)

items.question: What should be done if a patient develops nausea, vomiting, or abdominal distention during EN?
options: Prescribe antiemetic and prokinetic drugs regularly
Switch to parenteral nutrition without delay
items.explanation: These problems can be managed by prescribing antiemetic and prokinetic drugs on a regular basis.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · EN THERAPY (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a purpose of enteral nutrition (EN) in the intensive care unit?
options: To meet the patient's macronutrient requirements
To infuse nutrients into the intestines to maintain barrier and immunologic functions
To manage abnormal fluid volume, electrolyte, and mineral requirements
To serve as a platform for later nutrient provision
items.explanation: EN is not designed to manage abnormal fluid volume, electrolyte, and mineral requirements.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · EN THERAPY (part 2)

items.question: What additional measures may be required when using standard EN products?
options: Intravenous fluid, electrolyte, and insulin therapy
items.explanation: Additional measures such as intravenous fluid, electrolyte, and insulin therapy may be required to maintain homeostasis.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · EN THERAPY (part 2)

items.question: What can cause failure to reach the nutritional goal with EN?
options: Immediate initiation of EN after fluid resuscitation
Gradual increase in EN delivery rate
items.explanation: Failure can be caused by intolerance to the administered formula.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · EN THERAPY (part 2)

items.question: Which vein catheters are generally avoided for PN due to higher complication rates?
items.explanation: Jugular or femoral vein catheters are avoided due to difficulty maintaining a dry, sterile dressing and higher complication rates.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · PN THERAPY (part 1)

items.question: What is the recommended initial daily glucose dose for PN to avoid hyperglycemia?
items.explanation: The initial dose of glucose should not exceed 200 g/d to prevent hyperglycemia.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · PN THERAPY (part 1)

items.question: What is a relative contraindication for commencing lipid infusions in PN?
items.explanation: Preexisting hypertriglyceridemia (>400 mg/dL) is a relative contraindication for lipid infusions.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · PN THERAPY (part 1)

items.question: For which patients is hypocaloric nutrition recommended during the first two weeks of PN?
options: Fat-sufficient or obese patients with ADM
items.explanation: Hypocaloric nutrition is recommended for fat-sufficient or obese patients with acute disease-related malnutrition during the first two weeks.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · PN THERAPY (part 1)

items.question: When should parenteral nutrition (PN) be discontinued in favor of enteral feeding?
options: Once a patient can tolerate one-half to two-thirds of their requirement by the enteral route and there is no mechanical or other barrier.
Immediately when the patient shows any interest in oral intake.
After 2 weeks regardless of the patient's ability to consume food orally.
Only after complete cessation of all medical treatments.
items.explanation: PN should be discontinued once a patient can tolerate one-half to two-thirds of their requirement by the enteral route and there is no mechanical or other barrier.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · PN THERAPY (part 2)

items.question: What is the most frequent metabolic complication of PN?
items.explanation: The most frequent metabolic complication of PN is hyperglycemia.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · PN THERAPY (part 2)

items.question: How can hypoglycemia be prevented when discontinuing high-glucose, non-insulin-containing PN?
options: By abruptly stopping the infusion and providing a snack.
By slowing the PN infusion rate to 50 mL/h for 1 or 2 h prior to stopping.
By increasing the insulin dose immediately before stopping PN.
By replacing PN with enteral nutrition without any glucose supplementation.
items.explanation: Hypoglycemia can be prevented by slowing the PN infusion rate to 50 mL/h for 1 or 2 h prior to stopping.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · PN THERAPY (part 2)

items.question: What is a potential complication of using hypocaloric amounts of glucose in PN?
items.explanation: Using hypocaloric amounts of glucose in PN can help prevent hypoglycemia.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · PN THERAPY (part 2)

items.question: When should soy-based lipid emulsions be avoided during PN therapy?
options: During the first week of PN
After the first month of PN
Only if the patient has a known allergy to soy
At any time, as they are universally contraindicated
items.explanation: Soy-based lipid emulsions should be avoided during the first week of PN.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · PN THERAPY (part 3) | SPECIAL CLINICAL SITUATIONS (part 1)

items.question: What is a recommended approach to protein provision in critically ill patients?
options: Match energy provision to energy expenditure
Provide high amounts of protein regardless of inflammation status
Avoid matching energy provision to energy expenditure until systemic inflammation subsides
Use standard EN regimens without modification
items.explanation: Attempts to match energy provision to energy expenditure should be avoided as long as systemic inflammation remains severe.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · PN THERAPY (part 3) | SPECIAL CLINICAL SITUATIONS (part 1)

items.question: What is the most reliable indicator of functional iron deficiency in hospitalized patients?
items.explanation: A fasting transferrin saturation <20% is the most reliable indicator of functional iron deficiency.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · PN THERAPY (part 3) | SPECIAL CLINICAL SITUATIONS (part 1)

items.question: Why might clinical trials fail to show benefits from SNS in critical illness?
options: Because all critically ill patients are protein-catabolic
Due to the heterogeneity of critical illness and ethical concerns
Because nutritional interventions always improve outcomes
Owing to a lack of modern intensive care facilities
items.explanation: The failure is due to the heterogeneity of critical illness, ethical concerns, and rapid improvement or predetermined outcomes in some patients.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · PN THERAPY (part 3) | SPECIAL CLINICAL SITUATIONS (part 1)

items.question: In patients with renal failure, why should protein provision be increased when undergoing renal replacement therapy?
options: Renal replacement therapy increases the body's requirement for protein.
Renal replacement therapy removes large amounts of amino acids from the circulation.
Protein is needed to compensate for fluid loss during renal replacement therapy.
Increased protein intake prevents muscle atrophy in patients with renal failure.
items.explanation: Renal replacement therapy removes large amounts of amino acids, vitamins, and trace elements from the circulation, necessitating increased protein and micronutrient provision to compensate for these losses.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · SPECIAL CLINICAL SITUATIONS (part 2)

items.question: Why is zinc supplementation necessary in patients with high-volume chronic diarrhea?
options: Zinc helps reduce the volume of diarrhea.
Zinc prevents bacterial infections associated with diarrhea.
Patients lose significant amounts of zinc through secretory diarrhea.
Zinc enhances the absorption of other nutrients lost during diarrhea.
items.explanation: One liter of secretory diarrhea contains ~12 mg of zinc, which needs to be replaced in addition to daily requirements to avoid deficiency.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · SPECIAL CLINICAL SITUATIONS (part 2)

items.question: What is a key consideration when providing parenteral nutrition support (PNS) for patients with severe hepatic disease?
options: Minimizing protein intake to prevent liver damage.
Providing generous energy and protein despite the risk of encephalopathy.
Avoiding any form of nutritional supplementation due to high risks.
Focusing solely on fluid balance without considering nutrition.
items.explanation: Patients with severe hepatic disease should receive generous SNS in energy and protein, despite the increased risk of hepatic encephalopathy, which can be mitigated by careful management.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · SPECIAL CLINICAL SITUATIONS (part 2)

items.question: Why is preoperative nutritional support not recommended to increase serum albumin concentrations?
options: Preoperative nutrition does not affect serum albumin levels.
Serum albumin cannot be increased through nutritional interventions.
The effect on serum albumin is only seen with prolonged (>7-10 days) nutritional support.
Nutritional support may decrease serum albumin concentrations.
items.explanation: Preoperative SNS will not increase serum albumin concentrations and should not be provided for more than 7–10 days with the expectation of doing so.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · SPECIAL CLINICAL SITUATIONS (part 2)

items.question: Which electrolyte deficiencies are dangerous during refeeding and should be monitored frequently?
items.explanation: Phosphorus, potassium, and magnesium deficiencies are dangerous during refeeding.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · SPECIAL CLINICAL SITUATIONS (part 3) | REFEEDING SYNDROME

items.question: What is a preventable complication of refeeding even with simple glucose infusions?
items.explanation: Acute thiamine deficiency encephalopathy is a preventable complication of refeeding.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · SPECIAL CLINICAL SITUATIONS (part 3) | REFEEDING SYNDROME

items.question: What can be minimized by severely limiting sodium provision and increasing carbohydrate provision slowly during refeeding?
items.explanation: Refeeding edema can be minimized by these measures.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · SPECIAL CLINICAL SITUATIONS (part 3) | REFEEDING SYNDROME

items.statement: Parenteral nutrition involves delivering nutrients through the gastrointestinal tract.
items.explanation: Parenteral nutrition is delivered intravenously, bypassing the gastrointestinal tract.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition

items.statement: Physicians should collaborate with clinical dietitians and specialized pharmacists in managing nutritional disorders.
items.explanation: The passage advises collaboration due to most physicians lacking formal clinical nutrition training.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition

items.statement: Negative energy balance reduces the efficiency of protein turnover and increases the dietary protein requirement.
items.explanation: During negative energy balance, such as therapeutic weight reduction, protein turnover becomes less efficient, increasing dietary protein needs.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · NUTRITIONAL PHYSIOLOGY (See Chaps. 343-345.) (part 1)

items.statement: Energy doses greater than 70% of total energy expenditure have a greater protein-sparing effect.
items.explanation: Energy doses >70% of total energy expenditure do not provide additional protein-sparing effects.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · NUTRITIONAL PHYSIOLOGY (See Chaps. 343-345.) (part 1)

items.statement: Physicians often assume that a regular hospital diet prevents micronutrient deficiencies in all patients.
items.explanation: This assumption is unwarranted, especially if the patient's nutritional status was deficient upon admission and remains so.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · NUTRITIONAL PHYSIOLOGY (See Chaps. 343-345.) (part 2)

items.statement: Systemic inflammation can increase the requirements for certain micronutrients.
items.explanation: Inflammation increases the requirements and redistributes some micronutrients into tissue stores.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · NUTRITIONAL PHYSIOLOGY (See Chaps. 343-345.) (part 2)

items.statement: A 50% reduction in body weight significantly compromises physiologic function.
items.explanation: A 50% reduction in body weight places young adults on the cusp of thermodynamic survival.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · MACRONUTRIENT MALNUTRITION SYNDROMES

items.statement: Starvation edema is caused by increased extracellular fluid volume due to semi-starvation.
items.explanation: Semi-starvation increases the ECF volume, which can cause starvation edema.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · MACRONUTRIENT MALNUTRITION SYNDROMES

items.statement: A BMI >17 rules out starvation disease.
items.explanation: Starvation disease can occur even with a normal or above-normal BMI due to residual obesity or expanded ECF volume.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · NUTRITIONAL DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.statement: Systemic inflammation is the fundamental distinction between SRM and CDM.
items.explanation: The presence or absence of systemic inflammation distinguishes SRM from CDM.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · NUTRITIONAL DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.statement: Appropriate nutrition will immediately normalize serum albumin concentration during systemic inflammation.
items.explanation: Normalization of serum albumin requires the subsidence of systemic inflammation and takes several weeks due to its half-life.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · NUTRITIONAL DIAGNOSIS (part 2)

items.statement: Urinary urea nitrogen accounts for approximately 85% of urinary nitrogen.
items.explanation: Urinary urea nitrogen typically makes up about 85% of the total urinary nitrogen.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · NUTRITIONAL DIAGNOSIS (part 2)

items.statement: Enteral nutrition maintains the digestive, absorptive, and immunologic barrier functions of the gastrointestinal tract.
items.explanation: Enteral Nutrition is safe and helps maintain the normal functions of the GI tract.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · SPECIALIZED NUTRITIONAL SUPPORT (part 1)

items.statement: Elemental and semi-elemental formulas are primarily used for patients with a functioning gastrointestinal tract.
items.explanation: These formulas are designed for patients with maldigestion and malabsorption, not necessarily those with a fully functional GI tract.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · SPECIALIZED NUTRITIONAL SUPPORT (part 1)

items.statement: Fish oil emulsions are infused at the same rate as other lipid emulsions.
items.explanation: Pure fish oil emulsions are infused at lower rates due to their longer chain length and slower tissue uptake.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · SPECIALIZED NUTRITIONAL SUPPORT (part 2)

items.statement: Ascorbic acid is stable in parenteral nutrition solutions when protected from light.
items.explanation: Ascorbic acid degrades spontaneously in PN solutions, even when protected from light.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · SPECIALIZED NUTRITIONAL SUPPORT (part 2)

items.subtopic: Indications, Selection, and Provision of Specialized Nutritional Support
items.statement: Most hospitalized patients require specialized nutritional support.
items.explanation: Most hospitalized patients do not require SNS as they can eat enough with appropriate management.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · Indications, Selection, and Provision of Specialized Nutritional Support

items.subtopic: Indications, Selection, and Provision of Specialized Nutritional Support
items.statement: Instrumental SNS is indicated when optimized voluntary nutritional support has failed.
items.explanation: Instrumental SNS is indeed indicated when optimized voluntary support is inappropriate, impractical, or has failed.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · Indications, Selection, and Provision of Specialized Nutritional Support

items.statement: A nasogastric tube feeding should commence at a rate of 100 mL/h.
items.explanation: Nasogastric tube feeding normally commences at 50 mL/h.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · EN THERAPY (part 1)

items.statement: Elemental formulas begin at a slower rate and progress more slowly than standard polymeric formulas.
items.explanation: Elemental formulas indeed start at a slower rate and advance more gradually.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · EN THERAPY (part 1)

items.statement: Standard EN products are designed to manage abnormal fluid volume, electrolyte, and mineral requirements.
items.explanation: Standard EN products are not designed for managing abnormal fluid volume, electrolyte, and mineral requirements.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · EN THERAPY (part 2)

items.statement: EN is recommended to be started soon after a critically ill patient has been fluid resuscitated and stabilized.
items.explanation: Current guidelines recommend starting EN soon after stabilization.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · EN THERAPY (part 2)

items.statement: Lipid infusions are necessary to prevent essential fatty acid deficiency in all patients receiving PN.
items.explanation: Lipid infusions are not needed for hypocaloric nutrition if patients have normal or excessive fat stores.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · PN THERAPY (part 1)

items.statement: Serum triglycerides should be measured before starting lipid infusions to check for hypertriglyceridemia.
items.explanation: Measuring serum triglycerides is necessary to detect preexisting hypertriglyceridemia, a relative contraindication.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · PN THERAPY (part 1)

items.statement: Discontinuing PN stimulates a patient's appetite.
items.explanation: Discontinuing PN does not stimulate appetite and may delay progression to full voluntary food consumption.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · PN THERAPY (part 2)

items.statement: Artefactual hyperglycemia can occur if blood samples are collected from a dual-port central venous catheter without care.
items.explanation: Intermixing of sample with PN solution in a dual-port catheter can falsely indicate hyperglycemia.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · PN THERAPY (part 2)

items.statement: Iron is added to PN mixtures.
items.explanation: Iron is not added to PN mixtures due to compatibility concerns.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · PN THERAPY (part 3) | SPECIAL CLINICAL SITUATIONS (part 1)

items.statement: Serum ferritin alone is a reliable indicator of iron deficiency in the presence of systemic inflammation.
items.explanation: The anemia of systemic inflammation confounds interpretation of serum ferritin as an indicator.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · PN THERAPY (part 3) | SPECIAL CLINICAL SITUATIONS (part 1)

items.statement: Intravenous iron is contraindicated during bacterial infections due to increased susceptibility to infections.
items.explanation: Intravenous iron should be delayed if there is known or suspected bacterial infection, as it can increase susceptibility to microbial infections.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · SPECIAL CLINICAL SITUATIONS (part 2)

items.statement: Physical activity has no impact on the nutritional needs of bedridden patients.
items.explanation: Reduced physical activity reduces appetite, and without exercise, bedridden patients will not increase their muscle mass despite full nutrient provision.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · SPECIAL CLINICAL SITUATIONS (part 2)

items.statement: EN or PN improves quality and length of life in patients with advanced dementia who show little interest in food.
items.explanation: There is no evidence that EN or PN improves quality or length of life in these patients.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · SPECIAL CLINICAL SITUATIONS (part 3) | REFEEDING SYNDROME

items.statement: Abrupt carbohydrate provision during refeeding can increase the metabolic rate and stimulate new cell synthesis.
items.explanation: Abrupt carbohydrate provision increases the downregulated metabolic rate and stimulates N retention, new cell synthesis.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · SPECIAL CLINICAL SITUATIONS (part 3) | REFEEDING SYNDROME

items.assertion: Systemic inflammation reduces the benefit of increased protein provision under hypocaloric conditions.
items.reason: A minimum energy dose, such as 50% of total energy expenditure, may help preserve active tissue during severe systemic inflammation.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why increased protein provision is less beneficial under hypocaloric conditions with systemic inflammation.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · NUTRITIONAL PHYSIOLOGY (See Chaps. 343-345.) (part 1)

items.assertion: Permissive underfeeding refers to reduced energy provision combined with increased protein.
items.reason: This approach is used to mitigate the protein-wasting effect of negative energy balance.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; permissive underfeeding involves deliberate underprovision of all nutrients, not just reduced energy with increased protein.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · NUTRITIONAL PHYSIOLOGY (See Chaps. 343-345.) (part 1)

items.assertion: Systemic inflammation reduces the reliability of standard laboratory tests for micronutrient status.
items.reason: It redistributes certain nutrients from the extracellular compartment into tissue stores.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the redistribution caused by systemic inflammation is the correct explanation.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · NUTRITIONAL PHYSIOLOGY (See Chaps. 343-345.) (part 2)

items.assertion: Chronic Disease-Related Malnutrition (CDM) is worsened by anorexia.
items.reason: Anorexia leads to inadequate food consumption, which drives the nutritional deficit in CDM.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · MACRONUTRIENT MALNUTRITION SYNDROMES

items.assertion: Acute Disease-Related Malnutrition (ADM) is primarily due to voluntary food intake.
items.reason: Voluntary food intake is usually impossible under conditions of ADM.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; ADM occurs when voluntary food intake is usually impossible.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · MACRONUTRIENT MALNUTRITION SYNDROMES

items.assertion: Generalized muscle loss is especially dangerous in ADM.
items.reason: It places patients at the cliff edge of lethal depletion of their active tissue store.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why generalized muscle loss is particularly dangerous in ADM.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · NUTRITIONAL DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.assertion: Visual BMI can be used to estimate a patient's normalized dry body weight.
items.reason: It accounts for obesity, edema, or ascites by evaluating muscle groups while discounting subcutaneous fat and edema.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains how visual BMI is used to estimate normalized dry body weight.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · NUTRITIONAL DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.assertion: Standard polymeric formulas are isotonic or nearly so.
items.reason: They provide 1000–2000 kcal and 50–70 g protein/L, requiring adequate pancreatic and intestinal enzyme function for digestion and absorption.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; the reason correctly explains why standard polymeric formulas are isotonic or nearly so.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · SPECIALIZED NUTRITIONAL SUPPORT (part 1)

items.assertion: Parenteral nutrition is infused into a peripheral vein in adults.
items.reason: Because of its high osmolarity and often large volume, parenteral nutrition requires central venous access.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; parenteral nutrition is infused into a central vein due to its high osmolarity.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · SPECIALIZED NUTRITIONAL SUPPORT (part 1)

items.subtopic: Indications, Selection, and Provision of Specialized Nutritional Support
items.assertion: Patients with terminal disease whose course will not be mitigated by SNS are candidates for optimized voluntary nutritional support.
items.reason: Terminal diseases with no mitigation from SNS do not benefit from specialized nutritional support.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; these patients are not candidates for optimized voluntary nutritional support.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · Indications, Selection, and Provision of Specialized Nutritional Support

items.assertion: Continuous enteral nutrition is often better tolerated than bolus feeding.
items.reason: Continuous EN reduces the risk of regurgitation and aspiration.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why continuous EN is preferred.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · EN THERAPY (part 1)

items.assertion: Diarrhea during enteral nutrition always indicates a need to switch to elemental formulas.
items.reason: Elemental formulas are indicated when diarrhea persists despite the use of fiber-enriched formulas and antidiarrheal drugs.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; there are no well-established indications for elemental formulas, but they may be tested empirically.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · EN THERAPY (part 1)

items.assertion: The initial dose of glucose in PN should not exceed 200 g/d.
items.reason: Exceeding this amount can lead to hyperglycemia and refeeding syndrome in susceptible patients.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why the initial glucose dose should not exceed 200 g/d.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · PN THERAPY (part 1)

items.assertion: Patients with acute disease-related malnutrition require >500 g of glucose daily.
items.reason: This amount is necessary to meet their energy needs during critical illness.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; most non-critically ill patients do not need more than 500 g glucose/d, and those with ADM should receive ≤350 g/d.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · PN THERAPY (part 1)

items.assertion: Cyclic PN may prevent or reduce the severity of intrahepatic cholestasis.
items.reason: Intrahepatic cholestasis occasionally develops after many weeks of continuous PN and is multifactorial in origin.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not directly explain why cyclic PN may prevent or reduce intrahepatic cholestasis.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · PN THERAPY (part 2)

items.assertion: Hypertriglyceridemia occurs when lipid infusion rates exceed plasma triglyceride clearance capacity.
items.reason: Sepsis, renal failure, and diabetes mellitus increase the risk of hypertriglyceridemia by reducing triglyceride clearance.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason is a correct explanation for why hypertriglyceridemia occurs.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · PN THERAPY (part 2)

items.assertion: Clinical trials often fail to show benefits from SNS in critical illness.
items.reason: Critical illness is extremely heterogeneous, and not every critically ill patient remains severely protein-catabolic for long.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains why clinical trials may fail to show benefits.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · PN THERAPY (part 3) | SPECIAL CLINICAL SITUATIONS (part 1)

items.assertion: Iron dextran is compatible with lipid emulsions.
items.reason: Iron dextran appears to be chemically compatible with aqueous solutions of amino acids and glucose.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because iron dextran is incompatible with lipid emulsions.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · PN THERAPY (part 3) | SPECIAL CLINICAL SITUATIONS (part 1)

items.assertion: Patients with severe hepatic disease should receive generous nutritional support.
items.reason: They are relatively intolerant to starvation and commonly have CDM when admitted to hospital.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why patients with severe hepatic disease require generous SNS.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · SPECIAL CLINICAL SITUATIONS (part 2)

items.assertion: Perioperative nutritional support should always be provided for more than 7–10 days.
items.reason: It is necessary to increase serum albumin concentrations significantly.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because perioperative SNS should not be expected to increase serum albumin if provided for >7–10 days; the reason is also false as it does not achieve this goal.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · SPECIAL CLINICAL SITUATIONS (part 2)

items.assertion: Refeeding syndrome can lead to left heart failure.
items.reason: It is caused by an abrupt increase of intravascular volume, increased cardiac demand on an atrophic left ventricle, and myocardial deficiencies of potassium, phosphorus, or magnesium.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Enteral and Parenteral Nutrition · SPECIAL CLINICAL SITUATIONS (part 3) | REFEEDING SYNDROME
